data.title: Sentiment Slumps On U.S. Stagflation Fears
data.description:
By James Knightley

Confidence plunges as households worry about inflation and jobs

The Consumer Confidence Index published by the University of Michigan dropped 6.2 points to 50.8 in early April, below the 53.8 consensus, with expectations falling 5.4 points and current conditions down 7.3. There was a marked deterioration in every subcomponent across all income, age and political affiliation demographics. The survey was conducted 25 March-8 April so the cut-off was just ahead of President Trump’s latest tariff announcement changes that switched more of the focus to China.

The big move was in price expectations, with 1Y ahead inflation predictions jumping to 6.7% from 5% and 5-10Y ahead now at 4.4% versus 4.1% in March. The bond market really doesn’t like this, and Treasury yields are spiking. It also likely limits the Federal Reserve’s ability to cut interest rates anytime soon despite the worrying outlook for consumer spending that today’s data underscores.

Prices, incomes and wealth are moving in the wrong direction

Consumer are facing problems on three key fronts: 1) tariffs will put up prices and squeeze spending power, 2) government spending cuts are raising concerns about jobs and entitlements, and 3) falling stock and bond markets are eroding household wealth. So that means prices, incomes and wealth are all moving against the household sector and it is understandable why they are feeling anxious.

Consumers expect unemployment to rise

Regarding expectations for unemployment, the balance held at +53% saying unemployment will rise over the next 12M. That is 67 percent expecting a rise (on a par with 2009 levels) less 14% expecting a fall. We have only been at these levels on four occasions in the past 50 years. This paints a terribly gloomy picture for the outlook for consumer spending, which accounts for 70% of all the spending in the US economy. Next week’s retail sales are likely to be strong as consumers apparently spent large in March to get ahead of tariffs based on auto, iPhone and consumer electronic sales evidence, but will likely crater in subsequent months as higher prices kick in. Recession risks are real.

Index reading for the question "Is government doing a good job fighting inflation and/or unemployment"?

Interestingly, President Trump appears to be getting much of the blame, with 67% of respondents saying the government is doing a "poor job" on fighting inflation and unemployment. Only 18% say it is doing a "good job". Those are 2008 GFC levels of unhappiness about the government's performance, and worse than when inflation spiked under President Biden's tenure in 2022.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Oakmark Global Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
First-quarter highlights

Top contributors

  • Lloyds Banking Group (LYG)
  • Prudential (PRU)
  • American International Group (AIG)

Top detractors

  • Alphabet Cl A (GOOG,GOOGL)
  • Diageo (DEO)
  • Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF)

New purchases

  • Airbnb Cl A (ABNB)
  • Brenntag (OTCPK:BNTGF)
  • Pernod Ricard (OTCPK:PDRDF)
  • Sysco Corporation (SYY)

Final sales

  • ETSY
  • Kroger (KR)
  • Novartis (NVO)

FIRST QUARTER, 2025

  • The Fund’s Investor Share Class returned 4.39% versus -1.79% for the MSCI World Index for the quarter, and 9.10% since inception versus 6.21% for the index over the same period.
  • At the sector level, the largest contributors to performance were financials and health care, while communication services and materials were the largest detractors.
  • Geographically, the top three country allocations are 53.7% in the U.S., 9.8% in Germany and 8.4% in the U.K. For the quarter, the U.S., the U.K. and Germany were the top contributors to relative performance. France was a top detractor from relative performance, in addition to Spain and Italy, which were not owned in the portfolio.
  • Emerging markets accounted for 3.2% of the portfolio.

TOP CONTRIBUTOR | DETRACTOR

Top contributor

Lloyds Banking Group was the top contributor during the quarter. The U.K.-headquartered diversified bank’s stock price rose throughout the quarter as it posted fiscal-year 2024 results where net-interest income modestly outperformed consensus expectations. In addition, Lloyds issued fiscal-year 2025 and 2026 guidance forecasting robust net-interest margin expansion and announced a 1.7 billion GBP (Great Britain Pound) share buyback. We continue to monitor the Motor Vehicle provision following the onerous appellate court ruling and are optimistic about a favorable ruling from the Supreme Court. In our view, the bank has a strong management team and a balance sheet with high levels of capital, liquidity and reserves which can help it unlock further value.

Top detractor

Alphabet was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered interactive media and services company’s stock price declined despite having posted fourth-quarter 2024 earnings that were in line with consensus expectations. Search revenue growth remained strong, and management reiterated that the new “AI Overviews” feature is driving higher engagement with comparable monetization. The one miss during the quarter was in the Cloud segment, where revenue grew 30% year-over-year but fell slightly short of consensus expectations. We believe the shortfall was largely due to short-term capacity constraints and that the long-term growth outlook for Google Cloud remains robust. We continue to see Alphabet as a collection of great businesses that can further benefit from the company’s world-class AI capabilities. With shares trading at just 15x our estimate of next year’s earnings per share, we believe the stock is meaningfully undervalued.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

New purchases

Airbnb is an online marketplace to list, discover and book unique accommodations worldwide. The company benefits from a strong network effect between its guests and hosts. We believe there is a long growth runway as global travel is an attractive market, and alternative accommodations have been taking share. We anticipate Airbnb will drive further growth by creating more valuable services for both sides of its network. This includes the potential for paid placement, which has created significant economic value for comparable marketplaces. In our view, management is aligned with shareholders and well qualified to lead Airbnb as the company attempts to capture these growth opportunities. Short-term concerns about the macro travel environment and declining margins stemming from growth investments allowed us to purchase shares at a discount to our estimate of business value.

Brenntag is a global leader in the production and distribution of specialty and commodity chemicals. In our view, Brenntag is an advantageously positioned specialty chemicals distributor thanks to its robust network of customers and suppliers, which is a market that has historically grown faster than broader chemical manufacturing. We believe this market is coming out of a cyclical trough with indicators suggesting better growth to come. Moreover, the company's management team is implementing a multi-year plan to reorganize the business structure to better align with the distinct needs of specialty and commodity markets, increase focus on value-over-volume, sharpen incentives, and trim the company’s cost structure. Recent temporary challenges in the business environment have weighed on Brenntag's share price, presenting us with the opportunity to initiate a position in the dominant industry player at a discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

Pernod Ricard is the second largest producer of premium spirits globally, with over 240 brands distributed across more than 160 markets. Importantly, the company generates most of its revenue from brown spirits, such as whiskey and Cognac, and has the highest exposure in the sector to this category, which is particularly attractive due to high barriers to entry driven by aged inventories. Pernod Ricard also has the most regionally diversified portfolio among peers, with leading scale in its key markets of India, China and France. This further strengthens barriers to entry and has provided a long history of attractive returns and healthy cash conversion for the company. Additionally, we believe the company is poised to benefit from structural tailwinds stemming from premiumization trends and emerging markets growth where it has more exposure than its peers. Lastly, we like that Pernod Ricard is still led by the company’s founding family, which is aligned with shareholders as they own 14% of the equity. We believe CEO Alexandre Ricard has a good track record and is committed to growing shareholder value by improving execution, operations and organizational culture, which we view as self-help opportunities to close the company's margin gap relative to peers. The company's share price has come under pressure alongside other spirits producers as a result of post-Covid-19 industry de-stocking and demand normalization, which provided an attractive entry point to invest in this above-average company at a below-average price.

Sysco Corporation is one of the largest food service distribution companies globally. We appreciate that food service distribution is a stable industry that has historically grown slightly faster than food spending. This is because of a continued secular shift to food away from home and that scale-advantaged companies, like Sysco, benefit from better procurement terms from suppliers and demonstrate high operational efficiency. Recently, growth in the company’s largest and most profitable customer segment, independent restaurants, has slowed, but Sysco is increasing headcount and changing compensation incentives in its salesforce, which we think will reinvigorate growth and prove beneficial for long-term shareholders. In our view, Sysco remains a high-quality company with a credible management team, so we were happy to purchase shares at a discount to peers and the company’s own historical trading multiple.

PORTFOLIO MANAGERS

(Year joined Harris | Oakmark)

David G. Herro, CFA (1992)

Eric Liu, CFA (2009)

Tony Coniaris, CFA (1999)

M. Colin Hudson, CFA (2005)

Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Since inception returns for the indexes are calculated based on the Investor Class inception date.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The performance data quoted represents past performance. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Total return includes change in share prices and, in each case, includes reinvestment of dividends and capital gain distributions. The investment return and principal value vary so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost.

The securities mentioned above comprise the following preliminary percentages of the Oakmark Global Fund’s total net assets as of 03/31/2025: Airbnb Cl A 1.5%, Alphabet Cl A 2.5%, American Intl Group 3.2%, Brenntag 1.9%, Diageo 2.0%,

Etsy 0%, Kering 2.1%, Kroger 0%, Lloyds Banking Group 2.3%,

Novartis 0%, Pernod Ricard 1.5%, Prudential 2.3% and Sysco 3.7%. Portfolio holdings are subject to change without notice and are not intended as recommendations of individual stocks.

Access the full list of holdings for the Oakmark Global Fund as of the most recent quarter-end.

The information, data, analyses, and opinions presented herein (including current investment themes, the portfolio managers’ research and investment process, and portfolio characteristics) are for informational purposes only and represent the investments and views of the portfolio managers and Harris Associates L.P. as of the date written and are subject to change and may change based on market and other conditions without notice.

This content is not a recommendation of or an offer to buy or sell a security and is not warranted to be correct, complete or accurate.

Certain comments herein are based on current expectations and are considered “forward-looking statements.” These forward looking statements reflect assumptions and analyses made by the portfolio managers and Harris Associates L.P. based on their experience and perception of historical trends, current conditions, expected future developments, and other factors they believe are relevant. Actual future results are subject to a number of investment and other risks and may prove to be different from expectations. Readers are cautioned not to place undue reliance on the forwardlooking statements.

The MSCI World Index (NET) is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the global equity market performance of developed markets. The index covers approximately 85% of the free floatadjusted market capitalization in each country. This benchmark calculates reinvested dividends net of withholding taxes. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

The MSCI All Country World Index (NET) is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets. The index includes largeand mid-sized stocks and covers approximately 85% of the global equity opportunity set. This benchmark calculates reinvested dividends net of withholding taxes. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

On occasion, Harris may determine, based on its analysis of a particular multi-national issuer, that a country classification different from MSCI best reflects the issuer’s country of investment risk. In these instances, reports with country weights and performance attribution will differ from reports using MSCI classifications. Harris uses its own country classifications in its reporting processes, and these classifications are reflected in the included materials.

The Fund’s portfolio tends to be invested in a relatively small number of stocks. As a result, the appreciation or depreciation of any one security held by the Fund will have a greater impact on the Fund’s net asset value than it would if the Fund invested in a larger number of securities. Although that strategy has the potential to generate attractive returns over time, it also increases the Fund’s volatility.

Investing in foreign securities presents risks that in some ways may be greater than U.S. investments. Those risks include: currency fluctuation; different regulation, accounting standards, trading practices and levels of available information; generally higher transaction costs; and political risks.

All information provided is as of 03/31/2025 unless otherwise specified.

Before investing in any Oakmark Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, management fees and other expenses. This and other important information is contained in a Fund's prospectus and summary prospectus. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing. For more information, please visit Oakmark.com or call 1-800-OAKMARK (1-800625-6275).

Natixis Distribution, LLC (Member FINRA | SIPC), a limited purpose broker-dealer and the distributor of various registered investment companies for which advisory services are provided by affiliates of Natixis Investment Managers, is a marketing agent for the Oakmark Funds.

data.title: Alaska Airlines' 2025 Just Got A Lot More Cloudy (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Just three months ago, I wrote in this Seeking Alpha article that I believed Alaska Airlines (NYSE:ALK) had a bright future in 2025. It is no surprise that stock markets have tanked over the past week and airline stocks have fallen at even greater rates than the market in general. The buy rating I made has turned into a 34% decline, almost three times the rate of decline for the S&P 500. I made similar buy ratings on other airlines that have fallen just as badly. Later in the first quarter, I started to issue hold ratings and even those were optimistically generous.

Airlines are always at the pointed end of the spear of economic uncertainty and market declines. Investors understand the current macroeconomic and geopolitical issues but the specifics of not just the airline industry but also for Alaska Airlines are probably less known. This article serves to not only reassess the elements of my previous article but also to help determine where ALK stands with respect to the rest of the airline industry.

Delta Air Lines (DAL) reported its first quarter 2025 financial results on Wednesday, April 9. DAL is almost always the first airline to report, so its financial releases garner lots of attention; its guidance and outlook is usually indicative of what investors will find with other airlines. Delta had already guided to a reduced first quarter profit and largely came close to its revised guidance. Its guidance, however, is what caught everyone's attention. Delta pulled its full-year guidance because it doesn’t have the certainty regarding how 2025 will play out. It says it is already seeing weakness in domestic coach demand, the same segment that has been problematic for the industry for two years – most of the post-Covid airline recovery period.

An Unfinished Covid Restructuring

Part of the reason for the challenges facing the airline industry – and specifically the domestic airline industry - is that significant federal grants were given to the industry during Covid – which started in a presidential election year. Airline employees are a mostly unionized workforce that are heavily concentrated in a number of key states; aid to airlines came in the form of reimbursement for retaining airline employee jobs that would otherwise have been at least temporarily removed. Aid was given under the justification that airlines needed to be able to respond to demand when it returned and, domestically, it began to return in force in early 2022 with international demand coming soon after. The end result was that the economic restructuring that a macroeconomic event such as Covid should have produced did not occur.

Further, airline labor costs soared as airlines had to hire new workers to replace many skilled pilots and mechanics that reached retirement age or accepted retirement packages during Covid. Although demand returned, fares had to increase to cover the higher labor costs, and the industry largely has not yet reached a point of equilibrium between increased labor costs and increased productivity. Domestic economy traffic has suffered because of inflationary pressures that have eroded the spending power of many people in the bottom economic half of the U.S.

As of the end of 2024, ALK was one of just three large U.S. airlines that had a net profit margin greater than 2% with ALK’s 3.37% NI margin bested by United Airlines (UAL)’s 5.5% and DAL’s 5.9%.

ALK is essentially a hybrid carrier – with a lower cost structure than its other legacy carrier peers, but with domestic first class cabins that have been part of how carriers that offer multiple cabins have performed better than domestic competitors that have single class cabins on most of their aircraft. ALK does not presently have longhaul international flights or wide body longhaul aircraft under its own name but gained both of those as part of its merger with Hawaiian Airlines.

Mergers are Messy Affairs

The Alaska/Hawaiian merger was driven by ALK’s need to build scale in an industry where size and scope are key factors of success. ALK operated most of its domestic capacity from its Seattle hub and headquarters, but has smaller hubs up and down the West Coast. It gained a hub at Honolulu and flights across the North and South Pacific via Hawaiian.

Airline mergers are inherently messy and have a fairly high rate of failure when measured by the amount of profit growth and retention of the merged network. Indeed, Alaska acquired Virgin America, a San Francisco-based startup airline that operated for about a decade until it was acquired by ALK in 2016. Alaska decided not to retain Virgin America’s reclining premium cabin seats that competed for upscale premium traffic in the transcontinental U.S. market, resulting in significant share loss in the markets that ALK acquired from Virgin America.

Virgin America was intended to expand Alaska’s network out of the Pacific Northwest and Alaska but faced tough competition from United at San Francisco and American, Delta and United at Los Angeles. Delta tried to convince ALK to become a feeder airline for DAL’s Seattle to Asia network but, with ALK’s decision not to partner further with Delta, DAL set up its own hub at Seattle. ALK carries about twice the number of passengers as DAL carries from SEA; ALK has far more frequencies and destinations, especially in the western U.S. while DAL’s advantage has been its network from SEA to the eastern U.S. and its international network. Delta is the largest international carrier at SEA and adds even more flights through its joint venture partners across the Atlantic and Pacific. The Hawaiian merger is an attempt by ALK to more effectively compete against DAL and UAL, at least in the western U.S.

Airline mergers involve a lengthy process of integrating workforces under federal labor law and of obtaining FAA approval to merge operations, given that airlines have very different and unique operating standards and protocols. Labor integration almost always involves pay increases for the combined workforce. ALK’s workforce has historically been paid less than the big 4 airlines – including Southwest – even though nearly all of ALK’s employees live in much more costly areas of the country. ALK is still early in the process of integrating its workforces. Operational integration is underway, but Hawaiian operates three aircraft types which ALK does not, and so that process will look much more like the multi-year process that other legacy carriers have experienced. The result is that efficiencies cannot be obtained and costs continue to grow while the two “sides of the house” continue to operate.

Alaska has already made significant changes to its network and flight schedules, increasing aircraft utilization and expanding connectivity between the Alaska and Hawaiian systems. There remain a number of opportunities it can explore, including attempting to regain the position that Virgin America once had in a number of east coast markets by using some of the widebody aircraft that have more premium cabins.

High Risk Expansion to Asia

One of ALK’s most significant moves is the announcement of its intention to add flights from Seattle to Tokyo and Seoul by partly moving some of the service that Hawaiian operated from Honolulu to those destinations. However, the Pacific has consistently been the least successful part of U.S. carrier networks. Over the past quarter-century, the U.S. airline industry was profitable on a cumulative basis for 18 of those 25 years, with Latin America (which includes the Caribbean and Central America) profitable for the same number of years. Cumulatively, U.S. airlines were profitable flying the Atlantic just half of those 25 years, while they were profitable across the Pacific for just 9 years. Delta (including its predecessor Northwest) and United traded places as the first and second-largest carriers across the Pacific during the first quarter of this century and were profitable for 13 of those 25 years, but Hawaiian was right at the industry average with only 9 years of transpacific profitability in a quarter-century. The odds are heavily stacked against ALK that it will be able to generate not just consistent profits flying the Pacific, but also profits high enough to invest in the products necessary to win the high-value customers which DAL and UAL have already won over on their global networks including to Asia.

Add in that Asia is heavily targeted in the tariff battle, and transpacific networks for all carriers will come under significant pressure. Cargo revenues are certain to decline. The big 3 countries of NE Asia (Japan, S. Korea and Taiwan) are reportedly aggressively approaching U.S. authorities to negotiate new trade agreements, recognizing the value of military cooperation with the U.S. for each of those countries. Tokyo is the primary connecting hub for American and United and their partners, while Delta and its partner Korean Airlines hub in Seoul. All of those countries serve as major connecting hubs to destinations deeper in Asia – and it is far from certain that other countries in Asia will be as successful in winning negotiations with the U.S. compared to the big 3 NE Asian countries.

Alaska is not presently part of any joint ventures, including in Japan or S. Korea, so ALK will be starting service to two cities against competitors that have established joint ventures in an environment of potentially significant rearrangements of trade. And ALK will be using Hawaiian’s A330 aircraft that does not have anywhere near the amenities that established transpacific carriers have on their fleets. Given the likelihood that trade negotiations will last 90 days or more, there will be enormous uncertainty in transpacific markets as ALK starts transpacific operations from Seattle.

Domestic Expansion is Highly Problematic Now

The final piece of ALK’s strategic plan involves shifting connecting traffic from Seattle to Portland, OR in order to be able to accommodate more connecting traffic for its international and Hawaii flights at SEA. Like UAL, ALK has guided to above industry average capacity growth for 2025 (among carriers that have provided capacity guidance); if the commentary given by DAL about softness in domestic coach traffic, it will be much more difficult for ALK or any airline to successfully add capacity. It is very possible that ALK, like DAL, will move to a fairly tight capacity environment in order to keep yields from following amidst expected economic weakness.

Unlike the larger network carriers, including DAL and UAL, ALK routes nearly all of its connecting traffic through Seattle. ALK will already be at a competitive disadvantage compared to the larger carriers in that its Asia flights will connect just through Seattle, while there are multiple gateways to Tokyo and Seoul on other airlines. Thus, trying to increase capacity in order to facilitate connections leads to a higher risk of depressing local market yields.

Too Many Moving Parts

Although Alaska Airlines possesses a strong management team, they had very lofty plans for 2025. In the midst of economic uncertainty, reducing the number of moving parts in a company’s strategy is an advantage, but that is contrary to what ALK has planned up to this point for 2025. Given the increased economic uncertainty, especially around trade which heavily involves Asia, it is prudent to pull ALK back to a HOLD, even If trade issues are resolved with minimal additional negative impact.

data.title: AudioEye Eyes EU Growth, But Tariffs And U.S. Spending Pressure May Dampen Spirits
data.description:
Investment Outlook

AudioEye, Inc. (NASDAQ:AEYE) recently reported its Q4 2024 financial results, missing revenue but matching earnings estimates.

I previously wrote about AEYE in March 2023 with a Hold outlook due to lengthening sales cycles.

While revenue growth has returned, macroeconomic clouds are forming due to the effects of tariffs and downward U.S. federal government spending pressures.

I remain a neutral Hold on AEYE on uncertain revenue growth and continued high investment in SG&A.

AudioEye’s Market And Approach

AudioEye operates in a highly niche market of accessibility monitoring and related compliance information for websites and agencies.

The firm’s main solutions include:

  • Website accessibility checker.

  • Color contrast checker.

  • Accessibility training.

  • Automated fixing.

  • Certification.

AEYE seeks clients via direct sales and marketing and through channel partners.

According to a 2021 Mordor Intelligence market research report, the worldwide market for web analytics was around $8 billion in 2025 and is expected to exceed $18.6 billion by 2030.

If achieved, this growth rate would be a robust CAGR of 18.5% from 2026 to 2030.

The primary reasons for this projected growth are an ongoing need for digital businesses to understand and maximize their online investments for revenue growth and cost efficiencies.

Also, changes by major platforms on limiting ad tracking have made attribution more difficult, forcing clients to rely more heavily on analytics and monitoring however they can obtain it.

The COVID-19 pandemic drove strong demand for analytics which has largely remained since many customers have been trained to purchase more goods and services online.

Mobile analytics has also been a significant growth driver since numerous device types have become primary ways for consumers to research and make purchases.

The Asia Pacific region is expected to grow at the fastest rate of growth from 2025 through 2030, as the chart shows here:

For AEYE’s specific market, worldwide demand for website accessibility software was an estimated $386 million in 2021 and was expected to exceed $790 million by 2028, per a Market Research Guru research report.

This growth would represent a CAGR of 10.9% from 2022 to 2028.

Major competitors in the industry include:

  • Siteimprove.

  • Google.

  • Deque.

  • DYNO Mapper.

  • Level Access.

  • Crownpeak.

  • Monsido.

  • DubBot.

  • Silktide.

The company divides revenue between two segments, with their respective contributions shown in the chart here:

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has grown more recently due to partner & marketplace expansion and from the European Accessibility Act compliance requirements. Operating income by quarter (red line) has dropped because of higher SG&A investments and one-time items.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has trended higher as a result of improved scalability. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have increased due to one-time legal and stock compensation items and continued investments. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have remained essentially flat in recent quarters.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have fallen recently from one-time litigation costs and stock compensation expenses.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP).

For balance sheet results, AEYE ended the quarter with $5.7 million in cash and equivalents and $6.8 million in total debt, all of which was long-term.

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was $2.6 million, and capital expenditures were $100,000.

The company paid $4.4 million in stock-based compensation, or SBC, in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was negative ($1.8 million).

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 12.5%.

The net dollar increase in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $4.7 million.

In the past 12 months, AEYE’s stock price has fallen by 24% vs. that of the benchmark iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF's (IGV) gain of 2.4%, and has shown far higher volatility, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a handy major financial and operating metrics table for AudioEye:

Metric

Amount

EV/Sales - FWD

Rev. Growth Estimate - FWD

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

(Source - Seeking Alpha).

The company's Rule of 40 performance has been poor, with a heavy operating loss dragging down performance, as shown in the table here:

Rule of 40 Performance (Unadjusted)

Q4 2024

Revenue Growth %

12.4%

Operating Margin

-24.7%

Total

-12.3%

(Source - Seeking Alpha).

Why I’m Neutral On AudioEye

AEYE has produced increasing revenue with the likelihood of additional revenue growth in the near term from demand in the EU from its Accessibility Act deadlines in June 2025.

The company also expects strong demand from the U.S. but gross retention rate (customer count retention) has remained around 90%, close to the median rate of 91% in 2023, per a report by SaaS Capital.

Management has not disclosed its net retention rate, which is generally not a great signal as they like to tout that figure when it is strong.

The company has recently initiated a $12.5 million share repurchase authorization on stronger free cash flow performance on low CapEx expenses.

The graphic below shows the frequency of various negative keywords during the most recent conference call between management and analysts:

The chart shows that management is expecting a strong potential for a recession or slowdown due to tariff policy changes and "federal budget cuts."

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing AEYE at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 3.6x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of 18% (per management) against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of around 16% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% as of December 2024.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 5.2x on April 7, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, AEYE is currently being valued by the market at a discount to the broader index as of April 7, 2025, despite a similar revenue growth level.

Looking ahead, while the EU appears to be a potential growth driver, the impact of the tariffs and U.S. federal government spending reductions could be meaningful to AEYE.

But management doesn’t expect significant return on its increasing go-to-market investments until the second half of 2025 at the earliest, and possibly not until 2026.

As such, a macroeconomic cloud hangs over the company's future revenue growth prospects even as it invests in growing its European sales force and in other marketing initiatives in the U.S.

So, my outlook for AEYE in the near term is a neutral Hold on revenue growth uncertainties coupled with a continued expense ramp in the EU.

data.title: Dollar General Remains A Discounted Play
data.description:
The last few months have not been a very good time for the market. Most companies have experienced a great deal of pain because of economic uncertainty brought on by tariffs and other trade related threats. However, one company that has held up nicely is none other than discount retailer Dollar General Corporation (NYSE:DG). In fact, since I upgraded the company to a ‘buy’ in January of this year, shares have skyrocketed by 22.5%. That's well above the 8.9% decline experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time.

The newest data provided by management shows that growth continues, though there have been some signs of weakness. Furthermore, even though shares are not as cheap as they were previously, they have remained attractively priced from a valuation standpoint. Expectations of continued growth on both the top and bottom lines indicate to me that the business should continue to fare well in the long run. So even though it might be a bumpy ride, I think that maintaining it as a ‘buy’ candidate is logical at this point in time.

Shares are still on sale

The most recent fundamental data that we have regarding Dollar General covers the last quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. According to management, sales for that window of time came in at $10.30 billion. That's 4.5% above the $9.86 billion the business reported just one year earlier. When you dig deeper into the data, you find that this was the result of two things. The primary contributor to this increase was a rise in the number of stores in operation from 19,986 to 20,594. That's a year-over-year growth of 3%.

On top of this, the company also saw same store sales growth of 1.3%. That compares to the 0.7% increase experienced in the final quarter of 2023 compared to the final quarter of 2022. This is where some of the weakness comes into play, though. While the firm did enjoy a 2.3% increase in the average transaction value at its stores, traffic actually contracted by 1.1% on a same store basis. This means that while overall sales are up, it is coming from fewer customers paying more. In this environment, which is defined already by a tight budgeted consumer, this is yet further evidence of weakness.

On the bottom line, the picture was more complicated. From the final quarter of 2023 to the final quarter of 2024, net profits for the company fell by more than half from $401.8 million to $191.2 million. This is in spite of the fact that a reduction in net debt from $6.62 billion to $5.31 billion, which I would say is a substantial reduction, allowed interest expense to fall from $77.2 million to $65.9 million. There was a slight contraction in the company's gross profit margin from 29.48% to 29.40%. But the real problem for it involved its selling, general, and administrative costs. Management claimed that the rise from 23.60% of sales to 26.55% was driven in part by higher retail labor costs, incentive compensation, repairs and maintenance expenses, depreciation and amortization, and technology related expenses. But the big problem here involved the $214 million of impairment charges associated with the company’s store portfolio optimization review.

Speaking of net debt, in the chart above you can see how it has declined over the last three years. That chart also shows other important balance sheet related metrics like Dollar General’s debt to equity ratio and its current ratio. While the latter of these has remained at the same level for two years now, the firm’s debt to equity ratio has improved thanks to the paydown of debt. This is certainly encouraging.

During the final quarter of last year, management initiated this review involving not only its flagship Dollar General branded stores, but also its pOpshelf bannered stores. This review culminated in the company deciding to close 96 Dollar General stores and 45 pOpshelf stores. It will keep six of its pOpshelf stores, but it will convert them into Dollar General stores, ultimately bringing an end to its pOpshelf concept. Total charges associated with this amounted to $232 million, $214 million of which involved impairment charges. It's unclear what the remaining $18 million of expenses fell under.

Net income was not the only part of the business to suffer. In fact, most profitability metrics worsened on a year-over-year basis. Operating cash flow fell from $949.8 million to $800.9 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $627.7 million to $666 million. However, EBITDA for the business worsened from $809.3 million to $774.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the 2024 fiscal year as a whole compared to 2023. Revenue jumped from $38.69 billion to $40.61 billion. Same store sales grew by 1.4%, driven mostly by a 1.1% rise in traffic and, to a lesser extent, by a 0.3% increase in its average transaction price. Despite this nice increase in revenue, the firm's bottom line worsened, plunging from $1.66 billion to $1.13 billion. A small portion of this was because of an uptick in shrinkage from $910.7 million to $928.9 million. For those not familiar with that term, shrinkage is essentially what management considers to be theft. Unfortunately, this has become a problem for the company in recent years. In 2022, the company had only $481 million in shrinkage. That amounted to 1.27% of sales. But by the end of 2024, it had increased to 2.29%. This may not seem like much. But when applied to the kind of revenue the company generated last year, it's an extra $414.2 million in pre-tax profits that investors are missing out on. Of course, the impairment charge that I mentioned already played a much larger role in the recent decline in profitability. As the aforementioned chart illustrates, all other profitability metrics, except for operating cash flow, worsened year over year in 2024.

When it comes to 2025, management stated that revenue should climb by between 3.4% and 4.4%. Part of this growth will be because of same store sales growth of between 1.2% and 2.2%. But the biggest portion of the increase should be attributable to the addition of 575 new US stores and up to 15 new stores in Mexico. This all compares to the set of 125 new stores opened system-wide last year. But that's not all. In what has been called Project Elevate, Dollar General is focused on revitalizing its brand. This will see partial remodelings of 2,250 of its stores. The company will also fully remodel 2,000 locations on top of relocating 45 existing stores. This translates to a total of 4,885 real estate projects that the company will be working on. For context, last year, in addition to the 725 stores opened, the firm remodeled 1,621 stores and relocated 85 others.

This increase in revenue for the company should make possible a rise in profitability as well. Management currently anticipates earnings per share of between $5.10 and $5.80. This would mean net profits of about $1.20 billion, compared to the $1.13 billion reported for 2024. If we assume that other profitability metrics will rise at the same rate as this, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $2.58 billion and EBITDA of $3.10 billion.

Using these estimates provided by management, and also using the historical figures that the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Are multiples that I am generally bullish about. Furthermore, if you compare Dollar General to two other major discount retailers, as also shown in the aforementioned chart, you can see that our candidate is in the middle of the group when it comes to both the price to earnings approach and the price to operating cash flow approach. Only when it involves the EV to EBITDA multiple is Dollar General the most expensive of the three.

Longer term, management expects to achieve same store sales growth of between 2% and 3% per annum. Beginning next year, it also anticipates diluted earnings per share growth of 10% or greater. Using about 3% of net revenue effective this year, the company intends to grow its store count by around 2% annually. This will help, when combined with the aforementioned same store sales growth, the company in grow annual revenue by between 3.5% and 4%. Unfortunately, we could see some weakness because of broader economic concerns. In its final quarter earnings press conference for 2024, the management team at the enterprise did not really offer up much in the way of detail here. They did say that they believed that the firm is ‘well positioned to mitigate the impact’ of tariffs this year. They also stressed that they were able to successfully mitigate the tariff impacts that were endured in 2018 and 2019. It's unclear the extent to which the firm will have to raise prices for its customers. For instance, back in 2018 and 2019, the company did need to raise prices in some categories. However, management also acknowledged that its core customers are ‘already stressed’ from a financial perspective. And as such, the company is closely monitoring matters.

In the chart above, you can see share price performance for the enterprise since I last wrote about it earlier this year. At the start of this article, I mentioned that shares are up 22.5%. This chart shows an increase of 21.7%. This disparity is because of the timing of when my article was published compared to the timing used in the chart. But it's close enough. For context, that chart also shows how Dollar Tree (DLTR) and Five Below (FIVE) have performed over the same window of time. They have paled in comparison. This seems to indicate that the market is also currently in agreement with my opinion that Dollar General is perhaps the better candidate to keep in mind at this time. As other analysts have pointed out, including those at Citi Research, the company’s business model as one that focuses on delivering value for customers should help it to avoid significant pressure in this environment. When they decided to upgrade the company from a ‘sell’ to a ‘neutral’ (what Seeking Alpha designates as a ‘hold’), they did so while pointing out that only around 10% of the company's revenue is ultimately affected by tariffs. If true, this could help the company in many ways.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I am impressed with Dollar General. Honestly, I regret not buying shares for cheaper when I had the chance. Even though financial performance has been mixed as of late, with the bottom-line being particularly challenged, the long-term outlook is upbeat and shares are still trading on the cheap on an absolute basis. Given these factors, I have no problem keeping the company rated a ‘buy’ right now.

data.title: Evaluating Fibra UNO After Its Recent Outperformance
data.description:
Almost four months ago, I recommended holding Fibra UNO (OTCPK:FBASF) for its robust business model, its positive business momentum, and its exceptionally high dividend yield. I did not rate the stock as a “buy” due to its high debt load. Since that article, the stock has outperformed the market by a wide margin. More precisely, the stock has offered a total return of 4.5% whereas the S&P 500 and The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLRE) have declined 14% and 7%, respectively. After such striking outperformance, it is only natural to wonder whether the time has come for the shareholders of Fibra UNO to take their profits or if they should continue holding the stock.

Business Overview

Fibra UNO is the first and largest REIT in Mexico, with 613 properties in its portfolio.

Source: Investor Presentation for Q4-2024

Unlike other REITs, which are focused on one type of property, Fibra UNO is highly diversified. It generates 41% of its net operating income from retail properties, 33% from industrial properties, 14% from office properties and the remaining 12% from other properties.

The REIT reported its results for the fourth quarter in late February. Revenue grew 5% over the prior year’s quarter thanks to the growth of leasable area and rent hikes, which resulted from the pass-through of inflation to tenants. All the contracts of the company are indexed to inflation; this is paramount in the highly inflationary environment prevailing right now. Inflation in Mexico peaked at 5.6% a few months ago, and it is currently standing at 3.8%.

Moreover, Fibra UNO improved its occupancy rate sequentially from 95.3% to 95.6%, primarily thanks to an increase in the occupancy rate of retail properties from 92.7% to 93.7%. Notably, the industrial properties of the REIT had an impressive occupancy rate of 98.2%. The only weak point, as expected, was the occupancy of the office portfolio, which improved sequentially by 20 basis points but remained suppressed at 83.7%. Overall, thanks to improved occupancy rates and material rent hikes, Fibra UNO grew its net operating income and its funds from operations [FFO] per share by 3% and 10%, respectively, over the previous quarter.

The other major development since my previous article on the stock is the deteriorating economic landscape due to the aggressive tariffs that the new U.S. government has announced. China has already retaliated. If the other major trading partners of the U.S. retaliate too, then global economic growth is likely to be hurt.

On the bright side, Fibra UNO is much less affected than most companies, as it does not import any goods and hence it is not directly affected by tariffs. In addition, it is located in Mexico and hence it is less affected by tariffs than U.S. companies. This helps explain the striking outperformance of the stock vs. the S&P 500 and the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF, both of which include U.S. companies.

On the other hand, the above does not mean that Fibra UNO is immune to a potential global recession. If the ongoing trade war accentuates, it may cause a global economic slowdown or a recession. In such a case, Fibra UNO will probably experience lower demand for its properties and some of its tenants may struggle to pay for their rent obligations. Nevertheless, the REIT is highly diversified by quantity and types of tenants. It does business with several defensive tenants, such as Unilever, Walmart, PepsiCo, AT&T, etc. It also has a strong position in its business, as it has a market share of 11% in the office market, 11.5% in the retail market, and 10% in the industrial market of Mexico. To cut a long story short, Fibra UNO is not immune, but it is more resilient than most companies to a potential global recession.

Dividend – Debt

Fibra UNO has paid $0.11 of dividends in the last 12 months. This amount of dividends corresponds to an exceptionally high yield of 10.5%. However, the AFFO payout ratio is currently standing at 84%, which is elevated even for a REIT.

In addition, the company has a weak balance sheet. It has a leverage ratio (net debt to EBITDA) of 7.0x and its net debt (as per Buffett’s formula: Net debt = total liabilities – cash – receivables) is $7.7 billion. This amount is nearly twice as much as the market capitalization of the stock ($4.1 billion) and hence it is excessive. Moreover, interest expense consumes 53% of operating income.

As long as Fibra UNO maintains its solid business performance, it is not likely to have any problem servicing its debt and paying its generous dividend. However, if the drama with tariffs escalates, it may result in a global slowdown or a recession. In such a case, Fibra UNO may cut its dividend. On the bright side, management recently stated that it intends to reduce the leverage ratio from 7.0x to approximately 5.5x in the long run. Nevertheless, until management delivers on its pledge, the REIT will be vulnerable to a future downturn in its business.

The only positive aspect of a recession is the fact that interest rates will probably decrease in such a scenario. As a result, the REIT will be able to refinance its debt at lower interest rates, and thus it will probably enjoy a significant decrease in its interest expense.

Another potential risk related to tariffs is the adverse scenario of a resurgence of inflation. However, Fibra UNO is resilient to high inflation thanks to the inflation-indexed nature of all its leases. Therefore, if inflation increases significantly due to excessive tariffs, Fibra UNO will be much more defensive than most other companies.

Final Thoughts

Fibra UNO has outperformed the S&P 500 and its American peers in recent months, primarily thanks to its resilience to the deteriorating economic landscape amid excessive tariffs. As long as the economic outlook remains uncertain, the REIT is likely to continue outperforming the broad market. On the other hand, if the ongoing financial turmoil is followed by a severe recession, the REIT will probably have to cut its dividend, given its high debt load. Overall, thanks to its strong business model and its superior resilience (vs. most companies) to a potential recession, Fibra UNO maintains its hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: March PPI: Tariffs Push Up Intermediate Goods Costs
data.description:
Following the cold CPI release yesterday, the headline PPI fell more than expected in March, recording the largest decline in more than a year. The final demand indexes were heavily affected by energy prices and softening consumer demand, with little impact from tariffs. On the other hand, the intermediate demand indexes did show evidence that higher import duties have already pushed up costs in the early stages of the supply chain.

Headline PPI

The headline PPI recorded a decline of -0.4% MoM in March, well below expectations of an increase of 0.2% MoM and the largest decline since October 2023. The annual rate of the index cooled for the second straight month, down to 2.7% YoY, the lowest since September 2024. The decline in the index was driven by two segments:

  • Just as we saw energy have a significant impact on the CPI release yesterday, the energy index played a major role in the PPI release. The final demand energy index was down -4.0% MoM in March and, like the headline MoM move, it was the largest decline since October 2023. The BLS points out that “over three-fourths of the March decline is attributable to [the] -4.0% drop in the index for final demand energy.” More specifically, it attributes two-thirds of the decline to the -11.1% MoM drop in gas prices.

  • Within the services PPI segment, there was a large decline in the index tracking trade services (-0.7% MoM) which is associated with margins in wholesale and retail trade. This followed a large -1.1% MoM decline in February and caused the annual increase to fall to 3.1% YoY, the lowest since November 2024. The margin compression in March likely came as a result of the decline in consumer prices in March and doesn’t necessarily point to a decline in costs for producers.

Final Demand PPI

While the energy and trade services indexes played a major role in the decline in the PPI index in March, cost growth broadly was soft. Final demand PPI excluding foods, energy, and trade services was only up 0.1% MoM in March, slowing from three straight increases of 0.4% MoM. On an annual basis, the aggregate was up 3.4% YoY, down from 3.5% YoY and the lowest since June 2024.

One of the softest final demand segments in March (not including energy and trade services) was transportation and warehousing services. It fell -0.6% MoM, the largest decline since August 2024. The drop was caused by a large -3.9% MoM move in passenger transportation costs that is similar to the decline in airfares seen in the March CPI report. This was partially offset by a rise of 0.5% MoM in the cost of transporting foods which has a slightly higher weight. It’s likely that the decline in passenger transport costs was a result of lower fuel costs and weaker demand for travel as consumers backed off of discretionary spending.

Did the early tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China impact the final demand indexes? It doesn’t look like there was much of a reaction. The March data for final demand appears to have been more impacted by the weakness in consumer prices and consumer spending. This is most strongly supported by the move in trade services PPI. It is possible that the slight rise in goods transport and warehousing costs may be an early reflection of tariff costs entering the supply chain, but that is unclear.

Intermediate PPI

While the final demand indexes did not show much of an impact from the early tariffs, there were more interesting moves in the intermediate PPI indexes. These indexes are different from the PPI readings that make headlines as they show the change in costs earlier in the supply chain. Here are some intermediate segments to note in today’s release:

  • The PPI for materials and components for manufacturing jumped 1.0% MoM in March, the largest monthly increase since May 2022. A string of strong increases in Q1 2025 sent the index up 1.3% QoQ, the highest since Q2 2022. The new trend in the last three months suggests that this increase is at least somewhat linked to tariffs. Within the index, materials for durable manufacturing increased 1.4% MoM in February and 3.1% MoM in March. These unusually high gains likely represent a sudden increase in costs for the materials needed for auto & other transport goods manufacturing.

  • Data for March suggested that there was also an increase in materials and components required for construction. That index increased 0.7% MoM in March which is the largest rise since May 2022 as well. With Canada being a major source of lumber and Mexico a major source of gypsum, tariffs on these goods likely drove the increase seen here.
  • The BLS discusses some details of the rise in intermediate processed goods costs: “Among prices for processed goods for intermediate demand in March, the index for steel mill products rose 7.1% MoM. Prices for structural, architectural, and pre-engineered metal products; primary basic organic chemicals; industrial electric power; and plastic resins and materials also moved higher.” These were all offset by a -11.1% drop in gasoline prices.
Goods Inputs PPI by Production Stage Inputs for Goods Producers Inputs for Services Producers
Stage 1: Goods (ex-Food, Energy) +1.3% MoM +0.4% MoM
Stage 2: Goods (ex-Food, Energy) +0.8% MoM +0.2% MoM
Stage 3: Goods (ex-Food, Energy) +1.5% MoM +0.8% MoM
Stage 4: Goods (ex-Food, Energy) +0.5% MoM +0.3% MoM

The BLS breaks down PPI for intermediate goods by production stage. This breakdown shows that the largest increase in costs was earlier on in the supply chain (stage 1 is the earliest, and stage 4 is closest to final demand), and demonstrates the early impact of tariffs on US producers. It’s likely that these costs will continue to be pushed down the supply chain and eventually reach consumers.

Market Reaction

Once again, the markets were a bit distracted by headlines surrounding tariffs to have a big reaction to the PPI release. The largest move was in short-term yields as the United States 2-Year Bond Yield (US2Y) fell by about 2 bps. The United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) managed a minor downward move as well, but it was immediately reversed. The PPI headline move was a bit misleading because of the large impact of the energy index. The details in the intermediate indexes are more interesting and paint a more inflationary picture.

Overall, the March PPI report is summarized as a juxtaposition of an acceleration in intermediate goods cost growth and weakness in retail margins and a few other categories like passenger transportation costs that are related to softer demand. This also summarizes the balance of risks in the Fed’s inflation outlook that FOMC members will have to consider at the May meeting. The data today tempers my belief that the Fed can cut in May after yesterday’s cold CPI release, but it does not reverse it.

data.title: Stock Market Crash Does Not Foretell Recession Or Future Decline
data.description:
This time could be different, but this article will review data that includes the Cuban Missile Crisis, the Vietnam War, the inflationary 1970s, the double-dip recessions of the early 1980s, the Dot-Com Bubble and Covid-19. Every event is unique, so no one can be certain how the economy and the stock market will respond to the new tariffs. However, we have enough evidence to recommend chilling out.

A Stock Market Crash And Recession Risk

In 2022, I reviewed whether stock market drops foretold recessions. Using a 20% decline as a threshold for a bear market, here’s what I found. “The stock market did not drop this much from its previous peak around the recessions of 1953, 1957, 1960, 1980, 1981 or 1990. The stock market turned bear at the same time that the 1970 and 2020 recessions began, so it provided no early alert. It provided one month of advance notice for the 2001 recession. The stock market was three months late in its warning of the 1973 recession and eight months late in 2008. False warnings came in 1962, 1977 and 1987.”

Why would stocks drop 20% if not for a recession? A math example explains it. In the standard Dividend Discount Model, a stock is worth the sum of future dividends, discounted for the time value of money. A one dollar dividend, with a ten percent interest rate and a five percent expected growth rate, generates a stock market value of $20.00. If the stock analyst becomes pessimistic about the growth rate and cuts expectations for future growth from 5.00% down to 3.75%, the valuation of the stock goes from $20.00 to $16.00. A 20% drop in stock prices is the usual definition of a bear market. It can occur if long-term growth expectations are less optimistic, even if nobody expects a recession.

The key concept of the stock market is that today’s change reflects the change in expectations. A market decline does not necessarily imply that people are thinking the economy will get worse; they might simply think that past expectations were too optimistic.

Stock Market Crashes And Future Stock Prices

If a stock market decline does not forecast recession, does it at least forecast further stock market drops? Not at all. I looked at all instances since 1950 (when my data begin) of the Standard & Poor’s 500 index dropping at least as much as it did on April 4, six percent. There were 19 days of such a drop before the tariff-induced decline. I calculated the percent change in the index on subsequent days.

In the fifth day following a steep market drop, prices had increased 13 out of 19 times, achieving an average gain of three percent. Over the 30 days following the steep drop, again the market was up 13 out of 19 times, with an average rebound of four percent - not fully recovering, but moving up.

A full year after the day of steep decline, the market rose 16 out of 19 times, averaging a 24% rebound, which more than made up for the sharp drop. And ten years later, we had market gains in 15 out of 16 cases. (Three of the steep declines occurred during the 2020 pandemic, so we do not yet have 10-year results. As of this week, the market is 50% higher than its pre-pandemic peak.)

Will this year’s stock market drop be different? It certainly could be. Mr. Market offers no guarantees. But history suggests an implication for business leaders, and one for investors.

Business leaders should not assume that simply because of the stock market decline a recession is coming. But they should recognize that expectations for the future have changed. Perhaps people had been too exuberant in past months, or perhaps now they are unduly pessimistic. A person who had been highly optimistic when the market was booming in 2024 should now think of the market as a trusted friend who is saying, “Buddy, you’re ignoring important information.” But the person who watched last year’s stock market boom and thought, “Those kids on Wall Street are crazy,” can now feel more comfortable about that belief.

Investors who are panicked about their stocks should use this event to evaluate their investment strategy. They should not bail out of stocks simply because the market fell recently. Instead, they should consider their overall investment strategy. That includes investment goals, time horizon and tolerance for risk. Some people may determine that their strategy is fine and no changes are necessary. Others may want to adjust their asset allocation so that they sleep better at night. But it would be a mistake to sell stocks thinking, “I’ll get back into the market when it looks better.” It will, indeed, look better after it has gone up a good bit. At that time, the cautious investor has already missed the rebound.

Any asset allocation adjustment to reduce risk should be made with long-term goals in mind. A conservative investment strategy may not generate enough return over the long run for a comfortable retirement. If that’s the case, then trimming current expenses to increase savings should accompany the move to a more conservative portfolio. But if conservative investing will hit the goals and also provide psychological comfort, that’s a good approach.

We humans are emotional creatures. Whether that’s good or bad, it is who we are. But business and investment decisions are best made without emotions overwhelming us. A stock market crash can make us panicked in a way the hurts our decision-making. Stay calm and look at hard data.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Oakmark Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
First-quarter highlights

Top contributors

  • American International Group (AIG)
  • Intercontinental Exchange (ICE)
  • Deere and Company (DE)

Top detractors

  • Alphabet Cl A (GOOG,GOOGL)
  • General Motors (GM)
  • Merck (MRK)

New purchases

  • Constellation Brands Cl A (STZ)
  • Marathon Petroleum (MPC)

Final sales

  • There were no final sales during the quarter.

FIRST QUARTER, 2025

  • The Fund’s Investor Share Class (OAKMX) returned 1.14% versus -4.27% for the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) for the quarter, and 12.76% since inception versus 10.44% for the index over the same period.
  • At the sector level, the largest contributors to performance were financials and consumer staples, while consumer discretionary and communication services were the largest detractors.
  • As we have done in other periods when stock prices declined significantly, we are taking advantage of the opportunity to reposition the portfolio toward companies with the most attractive risk-adjusted returns.

TOP CONTRIBUTOR | DETRACTOR

Top contributor

American International Group (AIG) was the top contributor during the quarter. The company reported improved premium growth, strong underwriting results, and significant capital return during the period. We expect these trends to continue, which has the potential to lead to strong EPS (earnings per share) growth and improved returns. Over time, we expect the company’s return on equity to converge with peer levels. As returns continue to improve, we expect the valuation discount to the industry’s top peers to narrow.

Top detractor

Alphabet was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered company’s stock price declined despite having posted fourth-quarter 2024 earnings that were in line with consensus expectations. Search revenue growth remained strong, and management reiterated that the new “AI Overviews” feature is driving higher engagement with comparable monetization. The one miss during the quarter was in the Cloud segment, where revenue grew 30% year-over-year but fell slightly short of consensus expectations. We believe the shortfall was largely due to short-term capacity constraints and that the long-term growth outlook for Google Cloud remains robust. We continue to see Alphabet as a collection of great businesses that can further benefit from the company’s world class AI capabilities. With shares trading at just 15x our estimate of next year’s earnings per share, we believe the stock is meaningfully undervalued.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

New purchases

Marathon Petroleum is an integrated downstream energy company that refines, markets and transports petroleum products. Marathon commands the largest refining system in the United States with operations in attractive regions, which has provided meaningful cost advantages for the company and significant barriers to entry for competitors. In addition, we think Marathon's midstream business is an attractive asset thanks to its high market share in one of the most prolific gas fields in the world, which provides stability to the company's cash flows. Lastly, we appreciate management's focus on returns on invested capital and willingness to return capital to shareholders. Over the last year, refining industry margins have come under pressure due to a global wave of new supply during a period of soft demand. We believe industry margins have fallen below sustainable levels and that, in the long run, supply and demand will return to balance which should lead to higher refining margins for Marathon. Today’s short-term imbalance provided the opportunity to purchase shares at an attractive valuation relative to both current and mid-cycle earnings.

Constellation Brands is the leading imported beer company in the U.S. The company has a strong portfolio made up of iconic premium beer brands such as Modelo, Corona and Pacifico. We like that Constellation's beer segment has consistently outgrown the industry and believe it can further benefit from demographic tailwinds, new distribution points, and future price increases. More recently, the stock price has come under pressure due to both stockspecific and industry-wide challenges, some of which we believe will prove transitory. Despite the company’s strong historical performance and expectations for continued above-market beer growth, Constellation trades at a meaningful discount to other consumer packaged goods companies with similar growth outlooks. This dislocation afforded us the opportunity to initiate a position in the company at a significant discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

PORTFOLIO MANAGERS

(Year joined Harris | Oakmark)

Michael A. Nicolas, CFA (2013)

Expense ratios are as of the Fund’s most recent prospectus dated 1/28/2025, as amended and restated 1/31/2025 and 3/14/2025; actual expenses may vary. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Since inception returns for the indexes are calculated based on the Investor Class inception date.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The performance data quoted represents past performance. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Total return includes change in share prices and, in each case, includes reinvestment of dividends and capital gain distributions. The investment return and principal value vary so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost.

The securities mentioned above comprise the following percentages of the Oakmark Fund’s total net assets as of 03/31/2025: Alphabet Cl A 3.2%, American Intl Group 3.3%, Constellation Brands CL A 1.1%, Deere 2.7%, General Motors 2.8%, Intercontinental Exchange 2.8%, Marathon Petroleum 1.0% and Merck 2.0%. The price to earnings ratio (“P/E”) compares a company's current share price to its per-share earnings. It may also be known as the "price multiple" or "earnings multiple", and gives a general indication of how expensive or cheap a stock is. Investors should not base investment decisions on any single attribute or characteristic data point.

EPS refers to Earnings Per Share and is calculated by dividing total earnings by the number of shares outstanding.

The S&P 500 Index is a float-adjusted, capitalizationweighted index of 500 U.S. large-capitalization stocks representing all major industries. It is a widely recognized index of broad, U.S. equity market performance. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000® companies with lower price-tobook ratios and lower expected growth values. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

The Oakmark Fund’s portfolio tends to be invested in a relatively small number of stocks. As a result, the appreciation or depreciation of any one security held by the Fund will have a greater impact on the Fund’s net asset value than it would if the Fund invested in a larger number of securities. Although that strategy has the potential to generate attractive returns over time, it also increases the Fund’s volatility.
